entity_read_model.versioning: Read models evolve additively while preserving stable identifiers, lifecycle state semantics, parent bindings, and metadata envelope rules.

entity_write_model.versioning: Write models preserve slug, parent-binding, and desired-state semantics so future handlers can add optional fields without redefining the canonical mutation envelope.

lifecycle_event.versioning: Lifecycle events keep stable transition vocabulary and correlation semantics so audit, orchestration, and future event consumers can react without entity-specific parsing hacks.

effective_capability_resolution.versioning: Capability-resolution contracts evolve additively while preserving plan, quota, deployment-profile, provider-capability, and correlation semantics for tenant/workspace consumers.

slug_rules: Slugs are lowercase, URL-safe, and immutable after activation unless an explicit migration workflow is recorded.
Workspace, external application, service account, and managed resource slugs are unique within their parent boundary.

integrity_rules: Parent bindings are immutable after creation unless a dedicated migration workflow is recorded.
Descendant entities cannot remain active when an ancestor is suspended, deleted, or soft_deleted.
Soft delete is logical first: external identifiers stay reserved until an explicit purge workflow exists.
Metadata is additive and non-secret; credentials, tokens, and private keys live outside the canonical entity model.
Write models express desired state while lifecycle events capture the realized state transition.

entities.description: Human operator identity known by the platform and bound to tenant/workspace memberships.
business_rules: identitySubject is globally unique across credential sources.
Workspace memberships must point to workspaces inside one of the user's tenant memberships unless the actor holds a platform role.
Suspended or soft-deleted users cannot receive new membership grants.
platform_user.iamBinding.realm must stay bound to the platform realm and cannot be reused as a tenant end-user identity.
Self-service signup creates platform_user records in pending_activation until the effective signup policy resolves to auto_activate or a superadmin approves the account.
Password-recovery and credential-expiry UX may block console access even when the canonical platform_user state remains active.

Activating an approved pending_activation platform user must create or resume one idempotent initial-tenant bootstrap run that binds the user as tenant and workspace owner before console access is considered operational.

entities.description: Commercial and operational boundary for one customer account, plan, placement, and quota surface.
business_rules: placement must follow the hybrid PostgreSQL tenant placement policy from ADR 0002.
Tenant slug is globally unique and remains reserved after soft delete until a purge workflow exists.
A tenant cannot be activated until at least one ownership-capable platform or tenant administrator binding exists.
tenant.identityContext must declare the platform realm, tenant realm identifier, and realmStrategy before the tenant can transition to active.
tenant activation must provision or attach the configured Keycloak tenant realm context before workspace or end-user access is enabled.
Tenant activation triggered from signup approval must create the initial workspace, owner memberships, tenant IAM context, and baseline bootstrap resources inside one traceable provisioning run.
Tenant labels and tags are non-secret governance metadata; reserved namespaces remain operator-controlled and filters must stay scoped to the tenant boundary.
tenant.quotaProfile must expose tenant ceilings plus workspace subquotas; workspace subquotas may only narrow, never widen, the tenant-level effective limits.
Tenant deletion is logical first: descendant workspaces, memberships, identities, and managed resources must be suspended or marked deleted before a definitive purge can proceed.
Tenant purge requires elevated approval, reinforced confirmation, elapsed retention eligibility, and one recovery-oriented configuration export checkpoint recorded for audit.
Tenant inventory and functional-configuration export must stay secret-free, consistent, and recoverable enough to support operator reconciliation after suspension, deletion, or purge preparation.

entities.description: Tenant-owned delivery boundary for one runtime environment, resource portfolio, and delegated collaborators.
business_rules: Each workspace belongs to exactly one tenant and its tenantId is immutable after creation.
Workspace slug and displayName are each unique within the tenant boundary.
Workspace environment must align with the deployment topology environment catalog.
A workspace cannot move to active when its parent tenant is not active.
workspace.iamBoundary.clientNamespace must be unique within the tenant realm and namespaced from workspace slug metadata.
workspace.iamBoundary.keyPolicy must keep credential material reference-first and isolate secret paths per workspace.
Workspace apiSurface must resolve environment-safe control-plane, console, identity, realtime, and application base URLs.
resourceInheritance must describe whether logical resources are inherited from tenant defaults, cloned from another workspace, or specialized locally without duplicating tenant-shared resources.
The first workspace created during tenant activation must keep per-resource provisioning state so partial bootstrap failures can be retried without duplicating already-provisioned resources.

entities.description: Application registration owned by one workspace, exposing client metadata, redirect policy, and workload identity attachments.
business_rules: Each external application belongs to exactly one workspace and inherits its tenant boundary.
Redirect URIs must use approved workspace or public-domain patterns.
An external application cannot attach service accounts or managed resources from another workspace.
external_application.iamClient.clientId must be unique within the tenant realm and inherit the workspace client namespace.
external applications may register multiple Keycloak clients per tenant as long as every client stays bound to one workspace and one canonical application record.
external applications must declare supported authenticationFlows that remain compatible with protocol, client type, and tenant plan limits.
OIDC applications may only use approved login/logout redirect URIs and must keep post-logout redirects separate from interactive login callbacks.
SAML federation requires validated metadata or explicit single sign-on endpoints plus at least one signing certificate before activation.
Each federated provider alias must be unique within one external application and may only project approved attribute mappings into scopes, roles, or claims.
Starter templates for SPA, confidential backend, and B2B SAML patterns stay additive defaults and never bypass per-workspace validation or plan limits.
External applications may expose workspace-specific endpoint bindings, but each binding must stay isolated to one canonical application record and one workspace-owned credential set.

entities.description: Non-human workload identity used by one workspace for automation and least-privilege access to managed resources.
business_rules: Service accounts represent confidential Keycloak clients tied to one workspace and must never outlive the tenant/workspace namespace they belong to.
CredentialPolicy governs issuance, rotation windows, maximum concurrent credentials, and revocation evidence without exposing raw secrets in the canonical model.
Credential references are secret-free handles; only secret backends store raw material, and revocation must take effect before replacement credentials become active outside grace overlap.
Role bindings for machine identities may grant runtime BaaS access but cannot grant membership-administration privileges.

entities.description: Canonical registry entry for a provisioned workspace-owned backing resource such as a database, bucket, topic, or function.
business_rules: Managed resources represent tenant-safe canonical records for workspace-scoped BaaS assets and their provider references.
AccessPolicy must reference only workspace roles and service-account ids from the same workspace namespace.
Cross-product authorization for database, bucket, topic, and function resources is enforced by the shared accessPolicy plus the contextual authorization model.
Managed resources created by tenant bootstrap must persist provider reference lineage, per-resource provisioning status, and retry-safe attempt metadata for operator visibility.

Tenant-shared managed resources may serve multiple workspaces only when consumerWorkspaceIds, authorized application ids, and authorized service-account ids remain workspace-scoped and independently managed.

entities.description: Auditable binding between one platform user and one tenant role set.
business_rules: Tenant memberships bind one canonical platform user to exactly one tenant collaboration role (tenant_owner, tenant_admin, tenant_developer, or tenant_viewer) while preserving auditable Keycloak user/group provenance.
Tenant memberships never grant workspace runtime permissions on their own; workspace access must be resolved from independent workspace memberships inside the same tenant boundary.

PrincipalBindings and roleMappings must resolve only to identities inside the tenant realm selected by the tenant identity context, and any membership change must enqueue effective-permission recalculation for existing tenant-scoped resources.

entities.description: Auditable binding between one platform user and one workspace role set.
business_rules: Workspace memberships are granted independently from tenant memberships and may be sourced from direct user bindings, approved invitations, or Keycloak groups within the same tenant realm.
Resolved workspace roles must stay inside the owning workspace namespace even when group names overlap across workspaces, and tenant-wide roles must not be treated as implicit workspace grants.

RoleMappings capture precedence and sync status so operator-visible access changes remain auditable, reversible, and able to trigger effective-permission recalculation for existing workspace resources.

entities.description: Invitation record used to onboard a human user into tenant or workspace scope without storing raw secrets.
business_rules: Invitation acceptance is valid only while invitationStatus=pending, the acceptance token is still valid, and the verified actor matches the intended tenant or workspace target binding.
Workspace-target invitations must resolve to one tenant-safe workspaceId and must not mint cross-tenant memberships or substitute tenant-scoped roles for workspace-scoped grants.

Revocation or expiration permanently invalidates the invitation token; replay requires issuing a new canonical invitation record, and successful acceptance must enqueue effective-permission recalculation for the affected scope.
ExpirationPolicy drives default TTL, override eligibility, and audit evidence for tenant/workspace invitation decisions surfaced in the console and control API.

entities.description: Commercial plan that groups entitlements, quota policy, and one default deployment profile.
business_rules: Every plan must resolve to exactly one default deployment profile and one quota policy.
Retired plans remain readable for audit and grandfathering but cannot be assigned to new tenants.
Plan entitlements may only expose capabilities that are backed by the selected deployment profile and provider catalog.

entities.description: Reusable quota policy that defines default enforced limits and overage behavior.
business_rules: Quota policies must declare deterministic default limits for every governed metric.
Hard-stop quotas block new provisioning or scaling actions but preserve read and audit access.
Overrides require an audited policy workflow and must never widen another tenant or workspace boundary.

entities.description: Technical deployment profile that captures topology, environment support, and plane-specific defaults.
business_rules: Deployment profiles must preserve explicit control, data, identity, and observability plane separation.
Supported environments must align with the deployment-topology catalog.
Provider capability bindings are additive catalogs only; runtime secrets and credentials stay outside the profile.

entities.description: Provider-backed technical capability that can be exposed or constrained by deployment profiles and plans.
business_rules: A disabled provider capability cannot become effectively enabled even when a commercial plan grants it.
Capability metadata remains provider-specific but secret-free.
Provider capability catalogs must surface observable degradation and support level for operators.

integrity_rules: Membership grants are explicit and auditable.
Tenant membership may exist without workspace membership, but not the inverse unless a platform role applies.
Workspace membership must remain inside the user's resolved tenant boundary.
Workspace membership never implies platform-level authority.
Workspace tenantId must equal the parent tenant id.
Tenant suspension or soft delete blocks descendant activation.
External applications inherit tenantId from the parent workspace.
Workspace soft delete hides descendant applications before physical purge.
Service accounts inherit tenantId from the parent workspace.
Least-privilege role bindings are constrained by the workspace boundary.
Managed resources inherit tenantId from the parent workspace.
A managed resource may reference only same-workspace applications or service accounts.
Attachments stay inside one workspace.
Application automation identities remain explicit rather than inferred from resource ownership.
Application-to-resource links are explicit and workspace-scoped.
Soft-deleted applications cannot gain new managed-resource bindings.
Tenant membership tenantId must equal the parent tenant id.
Tenant suspension prevents new active tenant memberships until remediation is recorded.
Workspace membership workspaceId must equal the parent workspace id.
Workspace suspension blocks new active workspace memberships.
Invitations inherit tenant scope and cannot target another tenant.
Workspace-targeted invitations must reference a workspace under the same tenant.
Every plan must reference one quota policy.
Quota policy changes for a plan are audited as commercial governance events.
Every plan must reference one default deployment profile.
Plan and deployment-profile mappings change only through versioned governance workflows.
Deployment profiles list only provider capabilities that remain valid for their supported environments.
Capability degradations remain visible even when commercial plans continue to reference the feature.

lifecycle_transitions.summary: Allocate canonical identifiers and persist the entity before downstream provisioning side effects.
Make the entity operational after integrity, authorization, and parent-state checks pass.
Retain identifiers and ownership while preventing active use or new descendant activation.
Remove the entity from active listings while preserving auditability and identifier reservation.

business_invariants: tenant_id is the root business boundary for every non-platform entity.
workspace_id is required for external_application, service_account, and managed_resource lifecycle events.
Cross-tenant or cross-workspace references are invalid even if raw identifiers exist.
Platform users are the only human entity type; service accounts represent workload automation only.
Managed resource kinds map to authorization resource types and stay provider-agnostic at the canonical layer.
Membership and invitation records are first-class auditable entities and never rely on implicit role propagation.
Commercial plan entitlements must resolve through deployment profiles and provider capabilities before surfacing to tenants or workspaces.
Quota enforcement is safe-by-default: downgrades cannot silently strand live tenant usage without an explicit remediation path.
Capability-resolution outputs preserve plane labels so operators can distinguish control, data, identity, and observability effects.
Console operators authenticate in the platform realm while tenant end users, workspace clients, and machine identities resolve through tenant IAM contexts.
Tenant activation is not complete until the Keycloak realm/client-scope/mapper baseline required by the control plane has been reconciled.
Workspace application clients and service accounts are namespaced per workspace even when multiple workspaces share one tenant realm cluster.
Console signup, activation, suspension, and password-recovery flows must preserve the platform_user identity boundary and remain auditable under the platform realm.
Auto-signup behavior is policy-driven by global, environment, and plan overrides; rejection or approval decisions must be explainable to the operator.

Tenant ownership transfer requires a current tenant_owner to initiate the handoff, a designated target user to accept it before expiration, and no automatic superadmin escalation during the routine path.

Membership grants at tenant scope and workspace scope are evaluated independently; mixed-role access must be resolved by combining both scopes without leaking permissions across tenants or workspaces.
Any accepted invitation, membership mutation, revocation, suspension, or ownership transfer must enqueue effective-permission recalculation for already-provisioned resources in the affected scope.
Tenant labels, quotas, inventories, and export checkpoints remain tenant-scoped and must never leak cross-tenant identifiers or policy state.
Tenant deletion is logical first; definitive purge requires a retained audit trail, a recovery-oriented export checkpoint, elapsed retention, and elevated dual confirmation.

rules: Activation requires an existing user, tenant, and auditable grant decision.
Revoked memberships cannot be reactivated in place; a new grant record must be issued.
Workspace activation requires a tenant-safe parent membership or explicit platform override.
Workspace revocation does not implicitly revoke tenant membership, but access is removed immediately for the workspace.
Acceptance requires the acting identity to match the invitation target email hash or verified alias policy.
Expired or revoked invitations cannot mint memberships without a new invitation record.
Grandfathered plans stay assignable only to existing tenants already using the plan.
Retired plans remain readable for audit, billing reconciliation, and downgrade analysis.
Advisory thresholds emit observability signals before hard stops apply.
Blocked status prevents new provisioning or scale-out but preserves read-only, billing, and audit flows.
Pending activations represent self-service signups that still require policy evaluation or superadmin approval before console tokens can be minted.
Credential expiry is modeled as an authentication status overlay and does not bypass canonical suspension or soft-delete decisions.
CredentialStatus becomes rotation_due before hard expiry so a replacement credential can be issued and audited.
Revoked or expired credentials cannot be reactivated in place; issuance creates a new credential reference.
Rotation windows must respect workspace isolation and the configured credential policy.
Activation requires one ownership-capable binding, tenant identity context readiness, and one traceable provisioning baseline.
Suspension must propagate access controls to descendant workspaces, human identities, and service accounts without violating audit readability.
Deleted tenants keep slug reservations, quota evidence, and purge preconditions until an elevated purge workflow finalizes removal.
Activation requires the parent tenant to be active and all mandatory bootstrap resources to have converged.
Soft-deleted workspaces keep slug uniqueness reservations and clone lineage for auditability.
Only the current tenant owner may initiate the transfer, and only the designated target may accept it.
Accepted or expired ownership transfers cannot be replayed; a new transfer record must be issued for another attempt.
Any membership, invitation, or ownership mutation that changes access must enqueue recalculation before affected resources are considered converged.
Failed recalculations require an explicit retry or compensating grant decision before stale permissions may be considered remediated.

business_rules: MongoDB bootstrap remains tenant-safe by creating one logical database namespace per workspace and never exposing cluster-admin credentials through canonical contracts.
Document-database capability is profile-gated and may be omitted from lower tiers without changing tenant or workspace bootstrap identifiers.
All MongoDB bootstrap metadata must stay secret-free and auditable like the rest of the provider-capability catalog.

external_application_supported_flows.summary: Interactive browser and mobile login using authorization code + PKCE without a client secret.
Confidential backend or web application using authorization code and a client secret.
Machine-to-machine access for backend automation without browser redirects.
Browser login initiated by the service provider and completed against a SAML identity provider.
Partner-initiated SAML login with RelayState handling and signed assertions.

external_application_templates.name: SPA + OIDC PKCE starter
external_application_templates.summary: Starter shape for browser or mobile-first applications that rely on PKCE and front-channel logout.

external_application_templates.name: Confidential backend + OIDC starter
external_application_templates.summary: Starter shape for server-side applications that use client-secret backed authorization code and client-credentials flows.

external_application_templates.name: B2B SAML federation starter
external_application_templates.summary: Starter shape for enterprise partner federation with metadata validation, signed assertions, and workspace-role mapping.

notes: SAML federation is not available on starter.
Only PKCE-based OIDC browser flows are supported.
Confidential OIDC backends and machine-to-machine flows are enabled.
SAML federation remains unavailable on growth.
SAML is available for partner and regulated enterprise scenarios.
Enterprise enables IdP-initiated SAML and the full starter template catalog.
Large multi-provider federations are supported within the documented quotas.

initial_tenant_bootstrap_templates.description: Create or reconcile the tenant realm baseline, roles, scopes, and attributes required before user access is enabled.
Bind the activating user as the first tenant owner with auditable role mappings.
Create the initial workspace that hosts the bootstrap resource portfolio for the new tenant.
Bind the activating user as the first workspace owner for the initial workspace.
Provision the initial relational data store using the plan-selected shared or dedicated PostgreSQL baseline.
Provision the initial object-storage bucket required for assets, backups, and audit attachments.
Provision an optional document-database workspace baseline when the deployment profile exposes MongoDB.
Provision the initial Kafka-compatible topic when event capability is enabled for the plan/profile.
Provision an optional OpenWhisk action stub so the tenant can execute backend logic without manual first-run setup.

rules: A capability is effectively enabled only when it is granted by the plan, present in the deployment profile, and available in the provider capability catalog.
Workspace capability resolution inherits the tenant plan and may only narrow capabilities or quotas based on environment or provider availability.
Quota downgrades that would violate current usage must return a remediation-required outcome instead of silently applying destructive limits.
Control, data, identity, and observability planes remain explicit in the serialized result for operator inspection and audit trails.
Tenant quota serialization must keep tenant-level ceilings and workspace subquota reservations visible so operators can explain throttling, suspension, and downgrade blockers.
